Okay, you're up. Dan, what's the best model to implement paid tele seminars into a coaching program? I'm not sure I understand the question. Is the question to start with paid tele seminars and switch them to coaching? Is that the question? Correct, that's right. Oh, okay. First of all, I'm not sure that that's what you want to do at all. However, let me give you an analogy. Well, first of all, as I said today, the truth of the matter is there is no best model. There is a choice of models, one of which is best for a particular person in a particular market, in a particular set of circumstances, in a particular time and place. But I'll give you an analogy answer. Several box sellers, people who sell a kit, Rory started it, are having success with the kit being sold in three-monthly installment payments with free telecoaching during those three months. And then the telecoaching monthly charge is the same as was the installment for the kit. And so now the telecoaching continues has paid, after the kit has paid off in the first three months, with the charge uninterrupted, they're already used to seeing that charge on their credit card, and they're in telecoaching perpetually until it stops. So, until they stop it. So, if you use that as an analogy and you could make your tele seminars the same price as you wanted your coaching program to say, you could essentially bring them in on a series of, say, three tele seminars on three particular topics that are priced at X, and then really it seamlessly just moves into a continuing telecoaching program after that at the same price. So, anywhere you're pulling them from though, there's really no good reason I can think of to actually put the tele seminars in front of just selling them directly into the coaching program. If you were going to front end the tele seminars to the profession, has the front end offer, I only know of one doing it, that doesn't mean it's not being done a lot, but if you were going to do it, then you really get to sell the coaching program on the tele seminar. And so, the price of one to the other doesn't make any difference. If it's a series of three tele seminars, the bonus tele seminar might actually be the one where the coaching program is sold. You might have an application process and everybody on the tele seminar series is invited to apply for coaching. I don't know that there's any single answer to your question, but unless you're going to front end it, you may be being more convoluted than you need to be. Right. Okay. The second question is, how does a membership website work exactly? Beyond me, Bubba. Look, I had trouble getting a cup of coffee this morning. I have no earthly idea, not the guy to ask. By all means, start with Bill on a break and go from there. All right. When you're entering a brand, you feel where you're not well known and your strategy upfront is controversy to draw the attention. Where do you draw a line so that you don't shoot yourself in the foot? And an example would be, I came from a fitness world going into the golf world and I'm creating a series of products of what your pro doesn't know, what your pro doesn't know about the golf swing. So it's going to obviously upset golf pros. So where do you not get in trouble and shoot yourself? Well, see, I mean, once you've crossed that line, you know, I mean, once they're mad and presumably they're not going to be cooperative with you anyway, I don't know that there's another line. I mean, once you've crossed the first one, there's not much point in being subtle. I mean, how mad can they get? At some point their heads explode or something, I guess. You know, I don't know, but you know, I mean, once you've crossed the line, if you're going to take the position that I'm going to show you the stuff that your golf pro, who's been taking all that money from you all these years, refuses to show you and doesn't actually know and couldn't show you if you wanted to. Once you've crossed that line, I mean, go as far as you want. Yeah, they're going to be aggravated. And it's a fairly reliable positioning path to go down. It does, you know, it precludes joint ventures with golf pros. You know, that's what it does. And you know, the one thing you don't want to do is pick that fight with people who have a lot of media, you know, because they got more ink than you got. But I don't see the second line. How would you ever incorporate a celebrity golfer in something like that, or is it just a lost cause? No, it's perfect for that. Again, I don't really want to have a long conversation about levels of usage, but there's, you know, there's the celebrity that you actually taught now who's been paying golf pros for 30 years, could get a swing right and you fixed his swing and is willing to say so. That's it, like the furthest, highest, best, most utopian level of usage all the way down to the celebrity who's not going to say that, but is just going to say, you know, what so and so knows about golf swings, no pro I've ever worked with knows, or, you know, is the most interesting stuff I've ever seen in 20 years of playing golf. And you got choices. I mean, obviously you got pro golfers and golfers are just one of the amateur tour and retired pro golfers from the senior tour and, you know, you got those choices. I have a client in the commercial mortgage business, for example, who hired a fairly well known senior pro, senior tour pro to make two appearances a year at his client appreciation events and give him a photo and endorsement. And he can use all that stuff and he's paying a guy 20 grand. So that's like the bargain of the century. But you have other choices. See, you have a baseball player who plays a lot of golf, a football player who plays a lot of golf, a Hollywood celebrity who plays a lot of golf, a food network chef who plays a lot of golf, you know, you're not limited to pro golfers with that story. So let's take an actual example. Okay, so let's take Robert Wagner, who loves golf and plays in the celebrity program once a year. Wagner's going to cost you 100 Gs. And you're going to get picture and you're going to get at least some kind of statement. I don't think he'd care about offending golf pros. So the statement could be offensive to golf pros wouldn't make any difference. Get your picture taken with them get a little video clip of you showing him something about his golf game if you actually can improve his golf game. That'd be big because Bob bets on golf on his golf game. So if you actually could improve his golf game that'd be big so you take him out to get his picture shoot a video shoot a little audio you got to do it all one day, pay him 100 grand for the year you could probably get him tomorrow. And don't want to spend 100 now you got to start shopping right but somewhere there might be somebody who works for you who you could get for 10 or for 15 or for 20. Now you're not going to get Tiger Woods, you know, you're not going to get Phil Mickelson and you're not going to, you know, but there's a lot of choices who could work for that pitch and be interesting for that pitch. I think whatever it was. Yeah, my message was off at this late today. I'm not going to go to sleep but in any event one of the things I wanted to ask you there are kind of two different questions. You know the first quarter. What is it that you've seen in your group with different individuals? Okay, you've expanded it to a different question. See if you're asking me if I can give you a predictive criteria of winners versus losers I can't. Nobody's ever been able to do it and anybody who claims to be able to do it is full of crap. Because the exceptions are so profound they are the rule. If they weren't, you know, nobody from the ghetto would ever go to college and become a doctor. So trying to create a predictive model is in my opinion ludicrous. Now if you're asking how, given exposure to them, can you judge some commonalities? Yeah, I mean look, they are the, we're talking about it at lunch. So first of all, this is the most self-serving of all commonalities I can tell you. But the first commonality is they buy. They invest in education for themselves. My Pete Fernandez story. Here's how I met Pete in case you didn't know. We're exhibiting it for General Cassette at the National Speaker's Association Convention, so we're in the exhibit hall. And we got a bunch of our base cassette programs up on the wall. I didn't know Fernandez from Adam. Pete walks up to the booth. He looks, says, you guys sell them? So yeah, Carlos in the booth. He ends Carlos, gold American Express card. He says, I want one of everything. Now he didn't ask how much is everything. How many are everything? He asked, nothing. I want one of everything. I'll be back in about ten minutes, run the charge and you know, ship it to me. There's a reason Fernandez is successful. There's literally a closed loop. I mean Bill and I were talking about it at lunch. We were actually joking about coming over and smacking you. Because, see, Tucker buys everything. At most events, while the speaker is speaking, Tucker's a tobacco-room fellow in the order form, right? The DVD, CD thing of this meeting. Well, there's a closed loop between that. And I mean, it's horribly self-serving to say so, although you should say it to your people too. But because it is also true, right? So like the first indication, like in the old SuccessTrack seminars with Chiropractors and Dennis, I used to tell Foster all the time, I said, I can tell you by the time the seminar is over, who's got the worst car in the parking lot and who's got the best car in the parking lot. The guy that's got the best car in the parking lot is the one that bought first. The guy that's got the worst car in the parking lot is the guy that ain't buying at all. You know, you walk out and follow him. So one sort of behavior that evidences itself very quick is how they are about investing in themselves. Jim Rohn's lying about, you know, poor people have big TVs, big people have big libraries. So you walk into somebody's house, I can tell you a lot about that house by whether or not there's any books. And I mean, we have a friend right now who is frequently talking about becoming entrepreneurial, right? And I've said to Carl on a number of occasions, he's full of shit, right? Because walk around the house. Where's the stuff he's studying? There's no books, there's no tapes, there's no magazines, there's no nothing. Who's kid and who, right? Then there's a... So like what do they read, what do they listen to, right? How do they implement, how do they go from knowing it to making it happen in the physical world? How do you get them to do that? How do they do it? What do you see in the other words? What identities, what behavior is that? Well, I mean, the behavior of doing things is the behavior of doing things. I mean, there is not like any precursor step to that. What they read, what they talk about. So if their conversation disintegrates to snow tires and price of gasoline, you know, that tells you one thing. If their conversation on breaks at a coaching meeting, I can tell you a lot about people. And again, you can have 10 billionaires in a coaching group and there's a slow and a fast, right? So in my coaching groups, it's why I barred the laptops. It's because, so on a break now, there's like four of them who want them on a laptop, check an email. Another one's on a laptop showing somebody funny pictures. And another one's looking at a porn site. Now, I can tell you which platinum member or which porn site, which was that question. But I mean, now if I didn't know, it just so happens, the incomes match up perfectly. The guy making the least amount of money is the one who's looking at a porn site. The guy making the next least amount of money is the one who's looking at funny pictures showing everybody funny pictures. The guy checking his emails making the most money of the three, but the least money of everybody else in the room. So, I mean, as far as implementation, you know, somebody either does or they don't. And so I don't know that there's a predictive or a precursor commonality. They either do or they don't. And if at some point they switch from don't to do, it's you, in some cases, it's a cataclysmic event. In some cases, it's a sudden awakening. It's a change in who they hang out with, which we often facilitate. That's one of the things that coaching and mastermind groups and those sorts of things accomplishes. As you lift somebody out of the associative group they've been in and put them in a new associative group where everybody else is a doer. And all of a sudden from one meeting to the next, from one call to the next, they're like the only one who hasn't done anything. Well, that was acceptable behavior in the group they were in before. Now it's clearly unacceptable behavior. And in many cases that like jars them into action. But for the most part by the time we get them, there's some ingrained level of do it, don't do it. And see, first of all, I don't think we ever actually, we haven't seen the 80-20 rule in the 515-80. It hadn't changed in at least 50 years that I know of. So all of us, and all of the books and the tapes and the CDs and all of this stuff, I mean look, 20 years ago you couldn't find a self-help book. Now you go to parts of the old books everywhere, right? You don't have to go to bookstores on the friggin' internet. Oprah's doing it for God's sakes. I mean, all you gotta do is watch Oprah. You know, you don't even have to get off a couch if you're like, it comes to you. It's everywhere. And all this technology that's made it easier. So, I mean, you know, like there's faxes and emails and all this. The percentages haven't changed one damn wit. Follow any group of 100 people for 40 years to age 65, 1% is rich, 4% is financially secure, 15% have to keep working to survive, 80% are either dead or broke. It hadn't changed. First time I saw Social Security Administration stats for 1963. The last ones I saw were from 1993, no change. Think of everything that's changed in between, it hadn't changed damn things. So, what I say, the population keeps reordering itself all the time. Into 5, 15, 80 it does. So even in a coaching group, you got 20 in a coaching group, it reorders itself. Into 5% of them are kicking ass and taking names. 15% are like doing pretty well, right? 80% are pretty much wondering how in the hell the 20% are done. And so, I don't think we changed that. I think we facilitated to be faster, easier, better, bigger. We really helped winners win earlier, faster, bigger. We rarely changed a dedicated loser into a winner. Now, it may not require that, however, to delight them. But we don't really do it. And that's why, see, once I had my AMWI experience, the first, like the big aha that came out of that for me, is I didn't want to be in a business where my compensation was determined by someone else's performance. Where the compensation was based on turning chickens into eagles. I mean, I got it like the blinding flash of the obvious. I'm a 17-year-old kid and this guy is a 42-year-old mature adult business owner. He can't manage to show up at the opportunity meeting at his own house on time in order to let the guests in. This tells me this is no way to try and make your money. So, see, our business is all based on consumption, not performance. Performance happens. That's great. But otherwise, we're based on consumption. You buy it, you take it home, you come to the seminar, you go home, do something, don't do something, don't make any difference. I got the same amount of money. Every once in a while, I think, gee, it'd be great to get some more money from the ones who do something. But in order to do that, I'd have to change the whole formula and I don't like that, right? So, I don't think we make winners out of anybody. I think we help winners win bigger, faster, easier. That's what I think we do. Craig. I have a situation. Dan, I have a situation where I've had some of my guys nickin' bits and pieces of my coffee. And pretty much my mindset's been if they're applying, they've paid me a lot of money. I'm sort of tolerant of it. If they're in my coaching, I'm, you know, tolerant. And I wanted to know your mindset on that or your tolerance of threshold, because now I'm seeing guys who are like newsletter subscribers, nickin' the whole newsletter letter, you know, my newsletter for their newsletter. And I have a, that's not, I'm not tolerant of that at all. Um, it's a very difficult part of this business. You know, I'm actually more sort of, I mean, at least as sensitive about them stealing other people's stuff as I am about them stealing mine. You know. And again, I'd rather they go ask. And in most cases, everybody's like, yeah, go ahead. But you've put your finger on a real tough part of this business. And I think you'll never be happy because I'm never happy about it. I mean, I try, I mostly try and as you said, just ignore it and you know, mush on. But I don't think you can beat it other than situationally first being nice. And then in the cases where you really have a stubborn person who now knows you don't want them to do X and knows they shouldn't be doing X and they perpetuate in doing X now. If they're in your group, you got to throw them out. If they're a subscriber, you got to cut them off and you may have to formally cease and desist and you know, go through all of that stuff. But to my knowledge, nobody has a good answer for the question that you've just asked. We all deal with it literally on a day to day person to person, piece to piece sort of episodic incidental basis. So what you said, if you said you're never going to have a good answer for it, I guess the answer is to ignore it because you're always going to be unhappy and better ignore it and be happy. Well, you can't totally ignore it. Right. Right. Because the really egregious stuff has to be stopped. Right. Right. And by the way, it's why I always advise people to don't get involved in trademarking and patenting and all of that any more than you must. Your few key pieces of intellectual property, yes. But otherwise, you become the Bill McGowan line about MCI, you become a law firm with an antenna on the roof, you know. So companies like Disney, they have to. Right. And they got as many lawyers as they got elves and rabbits. And, you know, and they got to go after like everybody because otherwise they lose the right to protect. And that's not, you know, a fun way to do business or go through life. So we have, you know, William is a guy from a foreign company for the last few years. He's a good guy. He's been with us for all the time. And he shouldn't go back, you know, you like shaking hands and say, sure, this guy's gonna be a better guy. And maybe he did, maybe he's operating, maybe he's easier to apologize than it is to get permission theory. And he knows, I mean, he took the whole brand game, millionaires sales, whatever, the brand game, right now, and then he just used it. Now, he's in a foreign country. But, you know, so are we there. You know, these days, there is no foreign country. And, you know, you call them on it and it was like, hi, you're upset. Yeah, we're upset, you know, you can't do that. And he fixed it. But I mean, we watch it on a day to day basis. And, and sometimes, and again, sometimes stuff comes across and you kind of just like shrugging, well, okay, what the hell difference does it make, right? Or the guys like in, he's advertising in guns and ammo, you know, magazine, some kind of target practice kit, and he's used an entire block, you know, it egregious amount of my copy, but I kind of, you know, but he's in dentistry, now I care, you know. But man, it's a daily deal. And by the way, the more you do, the more visible you are, the more successful you are, the more of this you're going to face. The only other answer is, of course, to outrace them. You know, I mean, there's some parts of what we do that sort of gets locked in place and stays there, right? But a lot of what we do is pretty fluid. So then it's that part of the business is Ray Crox answer, we can invent faster, they can copy. So by the time they're doing what you were doing, you were doing something else, right? There is, it raises sort of a fun point that I'll mention very quickly. In some niches, copycats copy only what they can see, right? And they're too lazy to go look at everything so they only look at the most visible. So off and on over the years, I've had five or six or seven instances with clients where we have for a short period of time, Petaway money running really bad ads, right? Knowing that the moron knockoff artist who was stealing all our stuff would copy all those ads and lose his hind end. And we could afford it, he couldn't. And then he'd go away, you know? And we've done it on more than one occasion. And sometimes you do stuff like that, it's like discouragement to entry, you know, on a local basis. Mike Storms is in a karate school business years ago, he's in Meta-Ree, Louisiana. And he says there's already got like 14 competitors all within drive-in. He says these guys are coming over and I know they're opening two schools right down the road from me. And you know, you got any bright ideas? I said, yeah, I said why don't you rent like five of them billboards all the way down the street, you know, between your school and theirs for three months. Put your face up on them and Mike Storms karate. He says we're going to get any students? I said I'm not going to get a damn student. I said but it's really discouraging to the guy driving down the road thinking about opening up that little school over there when all he sees is your face all the way down the road. Maybe he'll think twice about this, right? Guy went away. Never opened the school. Okay, let's talk about how to get paid. I am on page 109. Some people get paid per hour. Okay, I would think that that I'm pretty convinced that's like the normal coaching model that is being taught by the normal coaching institutions who are kicking life coaches and business coaches out. At a rapid rate. And so kicking out coaches, not mentioning to them that you can't make any money in this business unless you have a way to get somebody to coach. So that's like problem number one. And then they're teaching them all the hourly model. For the most part, as I said at the very beginning of the seminar, it's no matter what you charge, you'll ultimately be unhappy because when you trade hours for dollars, you'll be able to get paid. You can only get paid for trade hours for dollars. You only have so many hours to trade and you can only get so much an hour when it doesn't matter what the value of the advice is that you're dispensing. The person is still watching a clock. They can see 45 minutes have gone by and now it's over and they're only good at, you know, at some point in time value, no value. It's just too big of a check to write for the hour. Some people do term programs. And usually there are two end outcome, very common in the get rich in real estate industry, for example, where there's a coaching program in the last 12 weeks and it's designed to get the person through and to end through their first transaction. And that's sort of the promise. Perry Marshall, the Google AdWords guy, has a term coaching program. It's X number of things over X number of weeks or months. I forget it's a set fee and it's done. Some people figure out a way to do coaching per project. Mostly that's consulting. It's not coaching, but some people have coaching groups that are about a project. There's a variation of it, which I'll talk to you about under how to get more money. But some people are doing per project coaching. Some people are doing the retainer stuff where they're paying by the month or they're paying by the year, essentially for access. And a lot of people screw that up by giving too much access, unrestricted access, uncontrolled access. Like coaching program includes you can email me and get questions answered. Well, 80% won't email you much, but the 20% who do could kill you. And you will always wind up out of every group with a few pen pals who you don't like want to write to you 8, 10, 12, 20 times a day. So that's this unrestricted access thing is generally not a good idea. If you do terms, the best thing is the automatically renewing term. It's okay. It's a 12 month or it's a 24 month or it's a 36 month program. And at the end of that period of time, it automatically renews and we take a pre-agreed upon money again. The stuff you're most commonly seeing in our world is the till forbid pricing formula where it's so much a month typically. A few people are so much a week. Stephen Oliver is a twice a month charge. So it's so much twice a month charged on the first and the 15th. And it is continuity till forbid. You got to scream. You got to get out. There's a lot of the traditional coaching is one on one delivery, meaning you're paying. It may be structured by a program, but you're paying for entirely or partially the right to talk to me on the phone personally to send me emails to send me faxes and or even to show up at my door and sit and talk to me. I have in most of the coaching stuff I've done, I have had always some one on one phone activity. Some people do it once and decide never to do it again because they find it too painful. And I understand. But it is a big value builder for people. It really adds to the value in the program. And it is a way to actually really help people, the ones who use it properly. So I've almost always had it. Every coaching program has some version of you got a time slot and you call and we talk. Now, just so you know about that, just as people are in coaching programs for different reasons, their behavior in coaching is very different from one person. And so in one on one coaching, you wind up with a lot of individual by individual by individual variance that doesn't surface as much in a group environment. So some people call totally unprepared for the call. They haven't given a thought before they jumped on the phone and they want to talk about the weather and who do you think's going to win the football game this Sunday. And they use up some of the time, half of the time, all the time doing that. Some people call the exact opposite. They're like thoroughly prepared. They've sent in the list ahead of time. If here's the five things I want to talk about, here's the order in which I want to talk about a man. If so, if we don't get to number five, that's okay. When we get to three, here's the three or four facts you need to know about each one of those. And man, I'm picking up the phone and they're on number two. And that's fine. Some people are calling for therapy. It's really not a business call at all. It's their kids, their wife, their employees, their mother-in-law, the relative they're supporting. Now that people know I got a dog, I got one guy that's asking me dog questions. I'm going, I don't know, I let mine do whatever it wants. That's my solution. Some people are, in my case, very marketing-oriented. Some people, the edges are very fuzzy. Some people call it for accountability. Some people don't want accountability. Some people call it for recognition. Some people don't want recognition. Some people miss their calls a little. Some people miss their calls a lot. Different people have different philosophies about that, by the way. Some coaching programs, the coach calls the person at a prescribed time. I've never done that, but there are coaching programs that do that. Some coaches, if somebody misses a call, they chase them. Some coaches, if somebody misses a call, they'll give them a make-up call. Personally, I don't do any of that. You ain't there tough. Then there's from multiple individuals, meaning group delivery. Now, that's the, certainly it's the most efficient. Oh, the other thing I should tell you about one-on-one calls is if you do do a fair amount of them, my advice to you is find like some way to like be entertained while you're doing it. Because after a while, just like staying with it can sometimes be a problem. So first of all, I told you, be careful about the order you put people in. So, you know, if you got a few problem children, you don't want like four problem children, one right after the other, because then you're, go be irritable. But like when the weather's good, I like to do, I like to sit outside part of the time on the deck. And, you know, I'll put the TV on with no sound and watch a couple horse races while I'm talking. I'll sort mail. The group delivery thing is of course the most time efficient has leverage in it, which one-on-one doesn't. Because theoretically you can put as many on a call as you want, or if you want them to have interaction, there's a number of programs that put them in groups. So there's buddies. There's six, eight, ten, twelve this hour, six, eight, ten, twelve the next hour, and it's like open lines. So it really is like six, eight, ten, twelve people sitting around a table, and having a mastermind meeting, except they're all on a phone with you. There's delivery where you're doing all the delivery. You're doing the one-on-one calls, you're doing the group calls, you're whatever. There's delivery where you're using other people to do some of it, most of it, all of it. How to get paid more? Well, number one, charge more. Secondly, select people who will give you more. The past three weeks I've had this conversation now twice with people who are customers who only pay X amount. I said, then go get better customers. I mean, it's that simple. Somewhere there's somebody. The same amount of work you're doing, and the same amount of expertise you got, and the same information you're given, there's ten people somewhere who'll give you three times as much money, because it's worth three times as much to them. Go find the people it's worth, you know, the most to. So charge more, select the clientele better. Number three, the third and the fourth, supply and demand and pent up demand, that helps push up price. So the way you get paid more is having fewer chairs than there are people who want them. Having an unlimited number of chairs and everybody knows it, or having a lot of chairs and everybody knows it, that suppresses price. But if there's only five chairs up here, and there's a hundred of you, these chairs become enormously valuable only because there's a hundred of you and five of these chairs. And so now the price for the chair goes up substantially. That's the principle of scalping tickets. The tickets are only worth $5,000 after 92,000 of the 92,400 seats have been sold at the regular prices. Now if you want to go to the Super Bowl, there's only these handful of seats left. Well now, price is no object, because somewhere there's some net width who'll pay $50,000 to go to the Super Bowl. I mean, so the last ticket is the most valuable ticket of all, because there's only one seat and there's more people left who want it. So this is about manipulating the ratio of chairs to people visibly or through copy or through your application and enrollment process. But they must see, if not physically in a room, they must see in their head that there are a lot less chairs than there are people who want them. They got to see it, they got to believe it, they got to understand it. So there's language about that. I mean, and you've seen some of it, I mean, okay, we're setting this invitation out to 4,172 people. In order to be fair, we're dropping it all at the same time at 8.01 a.m. at the same post office. We've set in all the FedExes on the same day. We've staggered the mail, so we dropped everything from the furthest zip codes today, tomorrow we're dropping the next stuff, the next day we're dropping the next stuff. And by the way, there's only 14 spaces. So obviously with 4,182 people getting the invitations and there's only 14 spaces, they're going to be gone on and on and on and on. Do you think I'm beating it up too much? No. You beat it up a lot because I want them to get the picture. If they're not going to physically actually see it in the room, I want them to get the picture of five chairs, 4,182 people. Oh, shit. And I'm in the back row, I better run. I may have to knock somebody down. That's the picture I want them to see. And so now the pipeline thing is sort of what we talked about here. I mean the ideal position to be in, well actually the ideal position to be in is not only to have a pipeline and pin up demand but to actually be reducing the number of chairs, not even keeping them static because that creates panic buying. So like this year, you'll find in one of the samples that I gave you or that we get to or whatever because I shrunk my groups again this year. So wheel out Platinum's to bid above the fee and nine bid above the fee, the highest of which was $71,000 for a 30-some-odd thousand dollar membership. Now, I wound up not taking the extra money but I could have. I could have. And so because they saw it. There's many people, they're all going to want those chairs. There's only so many chairs. Shit. I want a chair worse than that guy. I also got faxes of why I should take Charlie instead of Bill. You know, and here's the 17 bad things Bill said about you in the restroom that you didn't know about. And here's why Bill's a schmuck. And here's this and here's that. And you should take me instead. I got some of that stuff, which actually was entertaining more than it was anything else. But that's when you know you got it right, right? Is when they're fighting over the chairs. Now, pent-up demand is about pipelining. So it's about your ascension ladder narrowing enough that you always have more trying to move up than you got spaces for them to move up into. So there's either a waiting list or when you periodically announce open enrollment, if you will, days for this program, there's a virtual stampede to get it. And that's what you want to work on. Bundled goods and services levels and the ascension ladder. Here's the best thing. Here's the most important thing you want to know about all that. There's always 20% who will buy the premium version of whatever is being offered. If it makes any sense at all. There's 20% who will buy the premium version of what is being offered purely because it's the premium version. That's what they do. They will ascend to the next thing that is shown to them on the ladder because that's what they do. So therefore not offering a premium version of whatever it is that you are offering. Not offering another step on the ladder means you are letting 20% of the people give you less money than they would love to give you. If you offered them a reason to give it to you, that made any sense. Now sometimes that may be okay. Your whole base may not be big enough to justify putting another step on the ladder yet. You may not want to do the work of two levels or three levels or four levels. You may not want to bundle more stuff in, whatever. You've got to know that you are leaving the money from 20% of whatever population you are working with behind who would cheerfully and gladly give you more if they could. And now as soon as you've reshuffled them once, and now there's a bunch of people at this level and a bunch of people, as soon as you've done it, there's 20% again who would buy the premium premium version of your doohickey if you offered them a premium premium premium version of their doohickey. So two years ago with Platinum, so Platinum I think two years ago was 16,000 a year or 18,000 a year, I forget which, and limited to 18 members. And so how do I get some more money out of these guys? Well, I'm doing phone calls for my VIP groups anyway. I can fit a few more phone calls into the two days I'm doing the VIP groups, so I did Platinum Plus. You can be another 4,000 bucks a year and you can have a phone call on the same, because phone calls won't include, but you can have phone calls on the same day the gold VIP guys are having phone calls. So I don't know. I think I had five guys who moved to Platinum Plus. I mean, you know. So there's always, they're always ready to do it. They're always ready to buy the premium version. It's just a question of whether or not you want to offer it to them, but know that if you do not, you're leaving money behind. And remember about ladders. We talked about it very early. Everybody's conditioned to climb the ladder. You just got to show it to them. And they'll climb the ladder. Payment arrangements. Well, I've been all over the place on this over the years. Still vary it depending on the program. And, you know, I've done the, I don't want to screw around with payments at all. And I don't even want to screw around with credit cards. Write me a check or don't be in a group. I've done that. I've done payments. I've done three payments. I've done six pay. I've done 50% now and 50% after the first of the year when we actually start. I think you got to figure that out based on who you are selling to. The real thing to be careful about is not making the payment arrangements so easy that again you wind up having people as clients who you shouldn't have. See, what you never want is the person who is going down for the third time and looking to you for the life raft. Every once in a while you might actually be able to save one, but that ain't good business. That's good if you're running a charity or you're an evangelist or something. You should be doing that, but not in business because the odds aren't good. Most of the time they're going to be dragging you into the undertow with them. So if they're so weak that they can't pay for whatever it is that you're offering them with some reasonable accommodation. So let's say a $25,000 thing being sold to a dentist. He might not want to lay $25,000 on his AMX all at one time and we can kind of understand that and he might not want to write a check for $25,000 all at one time. But what would be too little? Well, $1,000 a month for 24 months. If you can't pay it off faster than that, you probably don't want. And in many cases what we're doing with people, they need to be able to invest some money to implement. So if they have to sell their paraplegic mother-in-law's wheelchair and mortgage the furniture and stuff in order just to get in, how are they going to do anything? Now, you might occasionally make an exception. And I have done it over the years. If somebody is really determined is in that situation and comes begging and convinces you that they are, you know, going to apply themselves and they, you know, now I'd never let them in for nothing and I would never discount. But I might play with their payment arrangements. But as a published or offered to everybody kind of thing, you got to find a point where not so demanding that nobody will give it to you or nobody could give it to you out of your clientele and market, but not so easy to pay for that anybody can, including people you shouldn't have in there in the first place. Exclusivity. Well, the most powerful lever you will ever have in selling coaching or consulting, either one, is again fewer chairs, right? Well, the fewest number of chairs you can ever put in front of a group of people is one because you can't sell no chairs. So the fewest number of chairs you can ever put in front of people is one. That's why exclusivity works is because there's only one chair. There's all kinds of exclusivity. There's one per business. So I'm only going to let one chiropractor in. I'm only going to let one dentist in. I'm only going to let one podiatrist in. I'm only going to let one retail store owner in. There's those kinds of exclusivity. There's geographic exclusivity. I'm only going to take one person from each zip code town, however you define an area. And that does answer an objection, by the way, all the time to, well, what happens if everybody in my town is using your stuff? Well, that objection is gone now because there's only one person in the town using your stuff, right? There's another way to answer that objection, too, which is how I used to answer. But the exclusivity makes it go away. The exclusivity can be by industry. It could be by size of business. It could be by geography. Any which way you cut it, as soon as you create it and you make that part of what you're selling, you gain an enormous amount of leverage because now you're holding up one stool. And there's only one stool. And if you don't get it, the guy across the street is going to get it, and you're going to see him sitting in the stool, and now you're really going to be unhappy. The other reason it works is an extension of that. As soon as you start to sell exclusive things, there's a buy it because they want it, and there's a buy it because they don't want that person to have it factor. And that is often as big a driving force as is the I want it. An example about all that, which I'll do as briefly as I can, is Larry King's story. But it'll stick it in your mind forever, so it's worth the retelling. Larry tells a story about how when he was on the radio in Miami, he's a radio host in Miami, he gets a call and it's a, as he says, it's an obvious bent nose. It's somebody of an Italian descent who explains that they're hosting a tribute dinner for the local Monsignor, and it would be ever so nice if Larry would emcee it as a favor. And these are people you don't decline favors to. So Larry goes to host the tribute. He arrives, they explained him in the back, there's a few things you ought to know. One, don't ask anybody to raise the lights. We're keeping the lights dim because there's some competitors in the room who don't generally like each other. There's some gentlemen from the FBI. And so we're just going to keep the lights dim. Secondly, you don't want to make any jokes about the mafia, not that there is such a thing, etc. And so Larry says he does the whole thing and it all was great and he's happy to be getting out of there. And the guy corners him in the back and says, you were great or waist-thrilled, wonderful, and you did us a favor. Now we owe you a favor. And he's like, no, no, no. He says, no, no, no, we owe you a favor and you got to understand we don't like owing people favors. So what do you want? And he says, no, I don't want anything. And so this goes back and forth. And finally Larry says he hears a question that you will probably never hear anytime in your life. But the guy looks at him and says, well, got anybody you want whacked? Now Larry says, no matter how moral a person you are, how spiritual a person you are, at the moment in time that you were asked that question by someone who clearly has the ability in order to deliver on the question, you begin making a list. Well, see now that's the premise of an exclusive. Because as soon as you put in front of people the premise that your peers, your competitors, people in your industry, people in your community, here's a list of 22 of MuQualify, and I'm only going to take one. They look at that list and there's somebody on there they can't stand. And the thought of them having the stool is, has motivating as is their desire for the stool, if not more so. So that's why exclusives work. The bad news about exclusives is you can only sell one stool. So you've got to be careful about it. I mean, if you say I'm only going to take one lawyer per area, the bad news is there's a lot more lawyers in San Diego than there are in Pocatello, Idaho. And so as soon as you take one, there's going to be 20 more you're going to be turning down and you're smacking your head. Oh, I could have had all that money. And then it takes you two years to find somebody in Pocatello, Idaho. That's the bad news. As soon as, let's say I did speakers and I said I'm only going to take one speaker from each category I'm going to work with. So one motivational person, one leadership person, one sales person, one marketing person. Good news is I'd have enormous leverage. The bad news is as soon as I got one of some, if it was a motivational person, the next day there'd be 40 more wanting to buy. And I got to say no to all of them. So there's a double-edged sword to this. Some people figure out how to split the hairs. I have a client, Dennis Tubbergen, in financial services. He's now up to six different area exclusive coaching programs. And so he's splitting the hairs by what kind of financial services they're delivering, by who they're delivering them to. So he's parsing it left, right, and front of center. Because he's built a system to sell this type of coaching plus area exclusive. So you think about, it's one of the ways to get paid more money for coaching. Because understand what it does. It immediately makes the chair infinitely more valuable without requiring any modifications to the chair. The value is from air alone, where the chair might have sold for $10,000 without exclusivity. It easily sells for $30,000 or $40,000 or $50,000 with exclusivity. And we didn't make any modifications to the chair. The last way to get paid more money. Oh, the other kind of exclusivity I want to talk about. The example I wanted to give you about project coaching. My client, Craig Proctor, who works with real estate agents, has had for a number of years a coaching program with a lot of people in it, sort of a middle swath program. I think it's 847 a month and he keeps hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of agents in it at any given time. A few years ago, he created a higher end program. His platinum group. It's originally supposed to have, I think, 10 or 12 people in it and now he's got like 50 or 60. But they are only one per area, so he is doing area exclusive with them. And then they are all getting a project per year. So two years ago, and they get me doing the project with Craig that they then get to use. So two years ago was the building of a shock and awe box, a big box for the real estate agent to deliver to someone who thought they might want to listen and sell their home. And so the platinum group gets to watch Craig and I figure it out and do it. They get to watch Craig test it and prove it and then they get the right to use it in their area. So the project is going on during the year. And so it's sort of a combination of look over your shoulder and then finished work product and an exclusive right to use. This year it was, actually I don't know what we're going to do this year. Last year it was a recruiting an inside agent system to fix that problem for them. So think about what's combined there that inflates the value dramatically. One is area exclusivity. The other is a look over your shoulder experience. And third is a work product. Fourth is now a finished thing that you get to use looping back only in your area, as well as the three coaching meetings and the calls, you know, and all of that. That adds an enormous amount of value. Now in reality it actually came about in reverse. In Craig's case it came about because he really needed to conquer the listing problem in his own real estate business and wanted the shock in all box for himself. But when it was going to cost him $150,000 plus points in order to have me build the shock in all box, he didn't want to write the check. So he figured out how to get everybody else to pay for it and get what he wanted. Now it actually turns out that it turned into a pretty good business and, you know, it's a multi-million dollar coaching business every year. Plus every year now he gets to pick something that he wants for his own business to get built with everybody else paying for it. So it's a very interesting example of stitching the pieces together in a way that creates a lot of value. How to get paid more as a consultant, which should be 110. The first three things go together. First of all, never accept predetermined assignments. You don't want to get hired to do X. You want to prescribe, right? You don't want the patient coming to you and saying, I want a hip replacement. Well, no, you want to be the one to do a bunch of diagnostics and say, you need a hip replacement and a head replacement and an elbow replacement, et cetera, et cetera. If you wish to do a lot of work, then it is easy for consultants to expand assignments. You sort of add on, add on, add on, add on, because the client's vision of what needs to be done and what he wants done and what is going to be done is usually pretty small. So, for example, Miracle originally came to us thinking they needed some new ads. They wound up with a two million dollar project encompassing all sorts of stuff. Now, they need it, but it's a far cry from what they had in their head of what they might need or want. My very first consultant client was a company called Brookside Labs. And Brookside Labs is a company, they're a consulting company. They sell agricultural productivity improvement to farmers. And by that, what I mean is they're really a soil analysis laboratory who calls their sales reps consultants. And so they come out and they charge the guy a program fee and they take dirt from all over his place and they put it a little vials and they bring it back to the lab and they test all the dirt and they figure out that he needs more iron over here and he needs more manganese over here and they make a color coded map of what kind of nutrition needs to go here, there, and there in order to make the dirt produce better. And then they go back and they deliver the map. So, I did a speech form and then we had the post-speech sort of debriefing, which is how I used to sell consulting. And so then their vision was, okay, I just did a speech. Well, it's obvious they're salespeople, they're not a sell. So the next thing they need is sales training. But really, so they kind of get that. In fact, that was like their question, it's okay, what do we do now to really get these guys trained? Well, that's okay, but even if we get them trained, say, they're also having trouble getting appointments. So we need a marketing system in order to get them appointments. Then they got this renewal problem, they're only renewing 30% of the clients, so they need a system over here to lock the guy in for three years or four years or five years. By the time I was done, I was there for 28 months, working on one thing, after the other, including arbitration between board members and all sorts of others. And the assignment just kept expanding and expanding and expanding and expanding. It should, because you can see things, they can't, obviously, that's one of the reasons that you're there. Always be in the strategy business, not in just a do-it business. So like from my standpoint, it's a copywriter. The reason, the big reason why most freelance copywriters don't make any money is because they take copywriting assignments. And so the client comes and says, I need an eight-page sales letter and an envelope and a coupon. What are you going to charge me to do that? The client comes to me and says, I need an eight-page sales letter and an envelope and a coupon. I say, you want a bet? I'll bet that ain't what you need at all. So now I'm in the strategy business. And I assure you what they need is not an eight-page sales letter and an envelope and a coupon. They need like a 48-step campaign, a new lead generation system, a TV infomercial, a shock in all box, et cetera, et cetera. Secondly, you make more money as a consultant when you just don't deliver advice, when you deliver work product. Some stuff, some kind of thing. So in Jack's theft control business, the consulting side of the business was really theft control audits and inventory control audits. But now we added to that train-to-trainer materials, constant video and DVDs that people sat in front of and watched in between the seminars. We added work product, training materials and so forth. Find a way for your services to be free. The easiest way to sell consulting is if you can convince everybody that it doesn't cost them anything in order to give you money. And if you're selling any kind of business improvement, that, of course, is relatively easy to do. It's all about making the money as a discount argument, which is usually tied to their transaction size. See, there's certain numbers you need to get a handle on in order to make a sale to somebody. You need to know their average transaction size. You need to know their average gross margin. If it's possible, you need some kind of customer value number, first year, one year, three year lifetime. With those three numbers, you can make almost anything you are selling them free because you can convince them. And the higher the transaction value, the easier it is, of course. See, to sell, so, for Charlie to sell a $25,000 program to a dentist, but if Charlie's average case value is 40, and their average case value is 10, he doesn't have to increase them to 40, but that's what they see. Oh, 40. All he's got to be able to do is double their average case value from 10 to 20, and in two and a half cases, they got all their money back. How many patients do you treat and how many cases do you get in a month? Four. Well, you got to have all your money back in 30 days. Like, free. I mean, why wouldn't you do this? Or how many more new, whatever's, do I have to get you as a result of being involved in this program? Often, if it's a $30,000 a year program and their transaction value is $1,000, 30. Well, that's not even three a month. That's 2.8 new customers a month you wouldn't have otherwise. Now, all I got to do is convince him that by being in this program, he's going to at least get 2.8 new customers he wouldn't have got otherwise. Generally speaking, it's not a hard case to make. People get it. You can make an argument for it. You can support it with your testimonies. So the three numbers you got to know in order to make a consulting sale or a coaching sale, for that matter, is you got to know what their average transaction size is. You got to know what their gross margin is. Although you'll try and use the gross transaction size as your measurement, but sometimes selling one-on-one consulting, somebody's actually smart enough to want to talk about margin. So you got to know what their gross margin is. And you got to know some customer value number. One year, first year, three year, lifetime. Obviously, the lifetime is the best because it will be the biggest. And that's a number, by the way, they won't know, but you can get them to guess at it. You can talk them through it. So the dry cleaner doesn't have any idea, for example. But he knows how much an average dry cleaning order is, and he knows how many times a year his average customer comes in. So now you can talk them through it. Well, how many customers do you think you keep for 10 years? How many customers do you think you keep for three years? And now you can calculate out what the customer value is, and now you can use that number against it. You can use that number to assist him in making an intelligent decision about participating in your program. So those are the three numbers that you need. When you go to escape billable hours, you sell projects, not time. You want other compensation than fees. Oh, yeah, you want to know. Don't just the next page. 111, I think. So you want fees plus other compensation. That's important. I said it and corrected myself. I didn't mean other compensation. Instead of fees, I meant fees plus other compensation. We'll come back and talk about that in a second. You want work product dollars because you'll always get more money if they're getting stuff than if they're just talking to you. You may be able to get materials in quantity dollars. This is especially true of corporate clients. So they got to buy one of your thing for all their salespeople or one of your thing for all their offices, etc. You may be able to get Mark Dwayne Huck Finn money. That means some part of what you deliver. You may be able to get somebody else to do the work for free for the glory, for the intern experience in order to get the testimonial. In order to be able to use the work product somewhere else, there's some kind of outsourcing in staff. So the five, six, and seven are the ways that you get paid and don't do the work. Recycling, real important. The longer you're in the gig, see one of the only places that there's leverage in consulting and copywriting, is dealing with the same type of client again and again and again so you're recycling the stuff you've already done. And so you build up a bank of knowledge, content, work product that can be pulled off the shelf, sold for the same price as if you were creating from scratch, but really it's 80% already done. Retained ownership, multiple use. This is pretty important. It's real important for copywriters. It's very important for anybody who's creating kind of work product is that, so if you create this whole system for hiring better people for your client in the electrical manufacturing industry, you want to retain the rights to sell that system to everybody, every place except electrical manufacturing. So now you've built a product. If you're dealing with a geographically restricted client, so let's say you create some kind of a system for a group of restaurants in Texas, you want to retain the rights to sell that same system to restaurants in every other state, but Texas. So you now get to bicycle the stuff across the country and resell it and resell it and resell it and resell it. Money from referred vendors, almost always when you do consulting work for somebody, you're going to need to connect them with goods and services you don't provide. You should get paid. Your choice whether you disclose it or not, but you should get paid. Resale of goods and services, meaning not only do they hire you as a consultant, but then they need a whole library of your stuff. And so you sell product at the same time. Now let's talk about other compensation structures. There's performance dollars and there's use fees. Performance dollars, one structure is, talked about it a couple times quickly and casually, is percentage above base. Percentage above base is a very easy thing to sell and a very difficult thing to retain clients with over a long period of time. So it has a positive and a negative. Percentage above base works like the very simple example is, okay, you made 100 grand this year, has a mortgage broker and there's a rate of inflation at 10% and your growth each year is 10%. So your base this year is 120,000 and your base next year would be 136,000 and the 20% to this year's base. And so you figure out the base for each one of the years and then you take a percentage of everything that occurs above the base. So now if I can get him when he's at 100 and I can take him to 300 and his base was 120, I had 280 of which I'm getting 30% of and ideal I'm getting it above some kind of a fee, not in lieu of a fee. There can be very big money made with percentage above base. The reversal of it is where you're saving people money. So I'm going to come in and find 52 ways to reduce costs in your factory and I'm going to get some agreed upon percentage of the reduction. We had a percentage above base thing in Jack Henry's business in theft control with a few large supermarket chains where two year contract, we establish your base theft rate and every half point we reduce it by we get X amount of dollars and these are big dollars. Now the negative of percentage above base is it usually creates an adversarial relationship that ultimately breeds resentment. Because in most cases the work you do for them is front and loaded. The results get better and better over time. So they are paying you more and more for less and less. And although they should be happy, at some point as they are getting out the checkbook to write the check this month for $52,000 and they haven't even talked to you in 90 days, they're grumpy. And so the first thing you ought to know about percentage above base is the longer the contract, the less likely you are not to have problems and to get paid. The one way to attempt to deal with that is with buyouts. So that there's a way for them at certain trigger points to cut you a lump sum check and terminate the relationship and continue to use everything they got during the point. Now you can sell against percentage above base for prepays, which is what we did in practice management. So we signed the doctor to the three year, 30% percentage above base contract and now we convince him that he's going to wind up paying us $412,000 over the next three years. But you can prepay your way out for $30,000. Well, and we were doing this, the interest rates, this is to be Carter, I think, the interest rates were sky high on a bank loan. But if you're discounting $412,000 to 30 grand, it doesn't matter what the interest rate is. It's irrelevant. So we wanted prepays. We didn't want percentage above base clients. We hated percentage above base clients because we knew what was going to happen. So we used it as a lever to get them into prepaying. So it was a staged sale. The first part of the sale was agree to the percentage above base contract. Well, they all easily agree to that, right? Because it's nothing. They don't have it. And they think at the moment, hey, if this guy gets me $200,000 or $300,000 over the three years, I'm happy to give him a third of it. I'm delighted. They won't be. But at the moment, they think, huh, who wouldn't love this deal? So they all signed that. That was like 100% close. Then the stage sale was sitting them down and setting their goals and making their chart and all that. And then having the realization appear before them of what that 30% was going to look like. And gee, you could make this all go away now for $36,000. And we got financing for the $36,000. So really, you could make it go away for $817.63 a month. Soup. And we converted them. So that was percentage above base. Royalties are another performance dollars thing. Again, very common, well, not very common, but the thing that top copywriters do, where you pay a fee plus you pay a percentage of gross for as long as you use the copy in order to sell things. Now, sort of the same thing occurs as with percentage above base in some cases. I mean, when I wind up with a client who is still paying me for something I wrote seven years ago, eight years ago, nine years ago, and every month they're writing a check and I haven't had to touch it since. Some don't mind. They have the right attitude about it. Others are grumpy about it. In some cases, tough. In some contracts, I put buyouts so they can pay a lump sum fee and make me go away. The per something is then some other way to get paid on performance. Number of people should showing up in seminar rooms. Reduction in absenteeism. I mean, anything that's measurable, you can tie a performance compensation to as long as you can measure it. The most important thing I will tell you about performance money is it should be fee plus performance money or at worst, fee has advanced against performance money like a salesman's draw. Not performance money only. Treat the performance money as gravy. Cover your time with the fees because there's too many ways for you not to get the performance money. Copywriter, I got clients I wrote stuff for four years ago. They've never printed it, never mailed it. And they spent a lot of money for it, but if I was relying on the performance dollars, not good. So the performance dollars to me are like gravy. I get them, I'm thrilled. That goes in a wealth account, but I ate off the fees. Use fees. There, there's licensing fees for work product, for training materials. You get to use my stuff and you pay a license fee in order to use it. There's renewables. You get to use this stuff and then you pay to keep using it, to keep using it, to keep using it. Again, there's per something. Anything that can be measured by use can be charged for by use. So number of salespeople who come into this room and watch these DVDs. And how can we control that? Well, the only way to keep modest and count the cookies. The counter cookies thing, by the way, is important. I got that from my theft control expert. Here's the counter cookies example. It takes five minutes to tell it. Mary's at home with little Johnny. Little Johnny and Mary, there are no ones in the house. Nobody else is there. No humans, no pets, no animals. Mary bakes 12 cookies. Mary is now going to walk out to the mail box, get the mail and come back. If she does that without the conversation that I'm about to have, I'm going to eat three, four, five, six, eight, nine, ten, twelve of the cookies while she's gone. So she has to say before she goes, Johnny, I'm going out to the mail box, I'm coming back. Right now we're going to count the cookies together. There are twelve of them. When I come back, the first thing I'm going to do when I walk in the door, before I read the mail, before I do anything else, is we're going to count the cookies again. And if there ain't twelve cookies here, because you and I are the only ones here and I'm not going to be here, so if there ain't twelve cookies here, your ass is getting whipped. If she don't have the conversation, the cookies are gone. So anytime you're getting paid based on use, you've got to have a way to count the cookies. So the Australian friend about his Europe Master License, I gave him the Count the Cookies mechanism, which is so now if we're going to get paid by the number of people who come into this room and watch these DVDs, when this person shows the DVDs, everybody who watches the DVDs has to get a packet of material and the packets have to be fulfilled from your office, not from the guys showing the DVDs. The DVD has to say everybody should have their packet of material and now turn to page eight in the packet of material. Maybe they got to go to, they get teleseminars and they got to have a number to log in on the teleseminar and the DVD has to tell them they get these three teleseminars. So now if he tries to show the DVD to people without the packets, everybody's saying where's my packet? He can't get the packets, only they can get the packets, so now you got to wait to count the cookies. If you don't have a way to count the cookies and you're getting paid per usage of anything, you will not get paid on the cookies. A lot of copywriters get paid on a number of pieces mailed. If you don't see the postal receipt, you haven't got a prayer, not a prayer. But even so, there's 600 ways to cheat with that. It's why I've never done it. The royalties, they can cheat on too, but there's a lot of ways you can police that. The postage thing, not so good. Equity. You will have, and the more visible you are, the more successful you are, the more this will happen to you. You will have people who will now want to offer you pieces of their business rather than any kind of other compensation in order to help them have you as a consultant, be in your coaching programs and so forth. The worst kinds of these, of course, are the startups. I've got a great idea. I have no money. Those are the two worst things you can ever hear from anybody. The I've got a great idea is bad enough by itself. The I've got no money is bad enough by itself. The two together, really ugly. And they will want to offer you the moon. Over the years, I've done two, passed up 200. Be happier if I passed up 202. I suppose somebody hits a home run this way, but while you are chasing that rabbit, you're leaving a lot of money behind. The second kind is they come to you, they already got to go on business. They already got some numbers and they got some kind of big expansion plan or growth plan or whatever, and now they want to offer you equity. Well, that's arguably more worth thinking about, but the biggest thing you should know about that, the short course on that, is equity in a privately held company in which you are a minority owner is worthless unless and until the company is sold. And so there's a couple of things if you insist on doing it that you want to think about. One is a trigger point at which the company must be sold if somebody offers X amount of dollars, or at that point if they want to turn it down, they got to give you your share of X amount of dollars. So if it's 10 million and somebody actually shows up and offers 10 million, and originally they thought they would grab that without thinking twice, but now eight years later they're happy, they're drawing big salaries, they got a plane, they got Mercedes, they don't want the 10 million. The contract says they got to take the 10 million, or they got to give you your 2 million, that's your 20% of the deal. The second thing you want to think about is controls on, they don't get money out unless you get money out. So salaries have to be controlled, because otherwise they, salaries can keep growing, the payments to their mistresses can keep growing, the company cars can keep growing, and you still don't see any money. So that's not good. So equity in privately held businesses in which you are a minority partner without a lot of contractual crap that controls how they get money, how you get money, when you get money, not very useful. Perks is useful. Quite a few consulting contracts offer the opportunity for various and sundry perks. A client years ago who did a lot of consulting for Bacardi, and Bacardi sponsors the annual penthouse photo shoot in Antigua, which is a nude photo shoot with 500 penthouse eligible women for three days. He got to go and hang out. Well, he would have worked for free, paid them, didn't make any, they were fine. So that's a nice perk. Nido years ago was doing a lot of consulting for furniture, company furnitures, this whole house at cost. You know, those sorts of things are available. There's no reason not to take them. You actually ought to look for them as a matter of fact.
